rationale: The two papers are tightly aligned on consumer locational choice in spatial environments: the citing study examines center-vs-edge location effects (including auditorium/seat contexts), while the cited paper develops a locational-choice framework for reserved seating based on proximity to others and focal elements. Given this strong conceptual overlap and the cited paper’s potential role as a foundational prior/model source, this is not an obvious incidental mention despite only DOI-level match evidence being available.
